data.title: Southside Bancshares gives mixed Q1 results
data.description:
• Southside Bancshares press release (SBSI): Q1 GAAP EPS of $0.78 beats by $0.03.
  • Revenue of $68.9M (+8.8% Y/Y) misses by $2.02M.
  • First quarter net income of $23.3 million;
  • First quarter earnings per diluted common share of $0.78;
  • Linked quarter loan growth of 2.7%;
  • Tax-equivalent net interest margin(1) linked quarter increased three basis points to 3.01%;
  • Annualized return on first quarter average assets of 1.10%;
  • Annualized return on first quarter average shareholders’ equity of 10.96% and average tangible common equity(1) of 14.39%; and
  • Nonperforming assets decreased to 0.11% of total assets.

data.title: Volkswagen Q1 profit falls 14%, signals mounting pressure amid weak demand
data.description:
• Volkswagen (VWAGY) reported a 14% decline in Q1 profitability, missing expectations as tariffs, weak demand, and geopolitical pressures weighed on performance.
  • The German carmaker's Q1 operating profit fell about 14% to €2.5B, below forecasts, and revenue dropped 2.5% to ~€75.7B, also missing estimates of €77.6B.
  • Operating margin came in at ~3.3%, highlighting profitability pressure. Weak demand in China and the U.S., alongside tariffs and rising competition, hit volumes and margins.
  • "The world is undergoing fundamental change – and we are aligning our strategy consistently. Wars, geopolitical tensions, trade barriers, stricter regulations, and intense competition are creating headwinds. In this challenging environment, we have managed to make tangible progress," said Oliver Blume, CEO.
  • For FY 2026, the company expects revenue growth of 0% to 3%, with an operating margin of 4.0% to 5.5%.
  • In its automotive division, the group forecasts an investment ratio of 11% to 12%, net cash flow of €3B to €6B, and net liquidity of €32B to €34B.

data.title: Tesla Semi finally begins mass production
data.description:
Tesla (TSLA) has announced its first Semi electric truck off its new high-volume production line at Gigafactory Nevada.

"First Semi off high volume line," the electric vehicle maker posted on X. According to its website, deliveries are expected to start this year.

Tesla (TSLA) first unveiled the truck back in 2017 and initially targeted production in 2019. It delayed its target thrice, before finally delivering a handful of units to PepsiCo in late 2022.

The Semi truck is priced at about $290,000 for the 500-mile range version and around $260,000 for the 325-mile range version. The truck is designed to travel up to 500 miles on a single charge. 

In comparison, Daimler's (DTRUY) electric truck Freightliner eCascadia is priced at over $400,000 and Volvo's (VLVLY) electric trucks cost between $276,000 and over $440,000.

data.title: Group 1 Automotive misses Q1 estimates
data.description:
• Group 1 Automotive press release (GPI): Q1 Non-GAAP EPS of $8.66 misses by $0.16.
  • Revenue of $5.4B (-1.8% Y/Y) misses by $20M.
  • Record quarterly U.K. gross profits of $230.6 million, a 6.3% increase over the comparable prior year quarter, driven by double digit same store parts and service and F&I growth.
  • Repurchased approximately 1.7% of the Company's outstanding common shares in first quarter of 2026

data.title: Unilever sees Q1 sales growth led by power brands, emerging market demand
data.description:
• Consumer goods major Unilever (UL) reported Q1 sales of €12.6B in Q1, beating estimates. Q1 topline fell 3.3% on a reported basis due to currency headwinds, while underlying growth was 3.8% in the three months to March 31, the company said in its trading update.
  • Unliver (UL) said the growth was broad-based across all business groups, with strong volume performance led by its Power Brands, which posted 5.0% underlying sales growth, including 4.0% from volumes.
  • Regionally, emerging markets continued to lead growth, with underlying sales rising 5.7%, including 4.2% from volume and 1.5% from price, with India delivering 7% growth.
  • Unilever expects FY2026 underlying sales growth at the low end of its 4%–6% range, with at least 2% volume growth. The company still sees a modest improvement in operating margin from 20.0% in 2025.
  • The company also declared a first-quarter interim dividend of €0.4664 per share and expects a modest improvement in underlying operating margin for the FY versus 20% in 2025.
  • UL stock traded 1.8% on Thursday during pre-market hours of trading.

data.title: Tradeweb expects 2026 adjusted expenses toward top half of $1.1B-$1.16B range as April trends toward a top 5 revenue month
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: Tradeweb Markets Inc. (TW) Q1 2026

Management View

  • "We delivered another record quarter surpassing $600 million in quarterly revenue for the first time in our history." (CEO & Director William Hult)
  • "Strong client activity and a risk-on environment drove 21.2% year-over-year revenue growth on a reported basis." (CEO & Director Hult)
  • "Our international business continued to set new records with 29% revenue growth" and "contributing to nearly 60% of our overall revenue growth." (CEO & Director Hult)
  • "Other revenues grew over 56% year-over-year, driven by our digital assets initiatives" and "market data revenues were down approximately 5% year-over-year" due to "a timing shift" under the amended LSEG agreement. (CEO & Director Hult)
  • "We now expect adjusted expenses to trend towards the top half of the initial guidance range of $1.1 billion to $1.16 billion." (Chief Financial Officer Sara Furber)
  • "From our work with the Canton network, to our new partnerships with Kalshi in prediction markets to crossover markets in crypto execution." (CEO & Director Hult)

Outlook

  • "We did see clients take a bit of a breather in April as they stepped back and recalibrated their forward strategies." (CEO & Director Hult)
  • "Looking ahead to April '26, despite a tougher comparison and a different volatility environment, we are trending toward another top 5 revenue month based on internal estimates." (CEO & Director Hult)
  • "Overall an average daily revenues are trending down by a low single-digit percentage relative to April 2025." (CEO & Director Hult)
  • "We now expect adjusted expenses to trend towards the top half of the initial guidance range of $1.1 billion to $1.16 billion." (Chief Financial Officer Furber)
  • Change from last quarter’s framing was expense pacing within the same range: "we will continue to invest in the business in 2026 and are expecting adjusted expenses to range between $1.1 billion and $1.16 billion" (Q4 2025) vs. "trend towards the top half" (Q1 2026). (Chief Financial Officer Furber)

Financial Results

  • "This quarter, we saw record revenues of $618 million" and "net interest income of approximately $17 million." (Chief Financial Officer Furber)
  • "We recorded $1.2 million in net loss this quarter, including $2.9 million of unrealized losses reflecting the mark-to-market of our Canton Coin Holdings." (Chief Financial Officer Furber)
  • "Other revenues of $10 million for the first quarter increased by 56%, primarily driven by growth in our digital initiatives related to our commercial relationship with the Canton network." (Chief Financial Officer Furber)
  • "Adjusted expenses for the first quarter increased 20.2% on a reported basis" and "headcount, which was up 11.4% year-over-year." (Chief Financial Officer Furber)
  • "We ended the first quarter in a strong position with approximately $1.9 billion in cash and cash equivalents" and "free cash flow exceeding $1 billion for the trailing 12 months." (Chief Financial Officer Furber)
  • "The Board declared a quarterly dividend of $0.14" and "we repurchased approximately 483,000 shares for $51 million" with "$523 million of aggregate share repurchase authorization remaining." (Chief Financial Officer Furber)

Q&A

  • Craig Siegenthaler, BofA Securities: asked about "the good versus bad volatility debate in the swaps market"; CEO Hult: "we are in how we would describe like a really kind of sweet spot for our business" and said March Europe volatility was "2x higher than the U.S. rates market" with markets "extremely orderly" and "request for market got up to like 45% of flow." (CEO & Director Hult)
  • Michael Cyprys, Morgan Stanley: asked about AI’s role and KPIs; CEO Hult: "We have our own kind of AI-powered assistant named, we're calling it, Terra" and "We're launching what we call AI Price 2.0 at the end of the second quarter." (CEO & Director Hult)
  • Simon Clinch, Rothschild & Co Redburn: asked about expense flexibility; CFO Furber: "roughly 55% of our expenses are fixed and 45% are variable or discretionary" and volume-tied costs are "like something like 3% of our total expense base." (Chief Financial Officer Furber)
  • Jeffrey Schmitt, William Blair: asked about EM growth; CEO Hult: "I think it was like 6% of our revenues in the first quarter of '26" and cited an "EM revenue wallet" of "a little bit over $1.5 billion annually." (CEO & Director Hult)
  • Patrick Moley, Piper Sandler: asked about Canton/DTCC pilot and tokenization; CEO Hult: tokenization is "an upgrade to market infrastructure" that can enable "real-time 24/7 collateral movement" and "It doesn't disintermediate us." (CEO & Director Hult)
  • Benjamin Budish, Barclays: asked about ICD cross-sell and balances; CFO Furber: ICD had "record revenues and balances" with "year-over-year growth around 8%" and said Asia is "a white space long term for us." (Chief Financial Officer Furber)
  • Alexander Blostein, Goldman Sachs: asked about Kalshi revenue opportunity and regulation; CEO Hult: "it's still early" and the near-term plan is "launching a free viewer in the second quarter" plus a "normalized API feed." (CEO & Director Hult)
  • Christopher Allen, KBW: asked about MAXEX partnership and mortgage outlook; CEO Hult: "It's early still" and described the partnership objective as "helping to expand institutional access across the U.S. residential private credit marketplace." (CEO & Director Hult)

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analysts: slightly positive, with growth-oriented probing on swaps volatility, AI, tokenization, ICD, and partnerships (e.g., "congrats on that 45% year-on-year growth" and "Super timely"); questions still pressed on regulatory uncertainty and execution details. (Craig Siegenthaler, BofA Securities; Alexander Blostein, Goldman Sachs)
  • Management: positive in prepared remarks and confident in Q&A, using reinforcing language such as "we are in how we would describe like a really kind of sweet spot for our business" and "we remain optimistic" on U.S. Treasuries and platform durability. (CEO & Director Hult)
  • Versus last quarter, management language shifted from "a constructive market environment taking shape" (Q4 2025) to emphasizing a faster-changing macro backdrop and April normalization: "That backdrop evolved quickly" and "clients take a bit of a breather in April." (CEO & Director Hult)

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • Revenue scale increased from "record revenues of $521 million" (Q4 2025) to "record revenues of $618 million" (Q1 2026). (Chief Financial Officer Furber)
  • Market data timing impact became a clearer headline in Q1: "revenue recognition... shifted to $2 million being recognized in the first month of every quarter" (Q1 2026) after the prior reference to an "$8 million boost" in January 2025 and the same shift described in Q4 context. (CEO & Director Hult)
  • Guidance language tightened on spending: Q4 said "adjusted expenses to range between $1.1 billion and $1.16 billion" while Q1 said "trend towards the top half" of that range. (Chief Financial Officer Furber)
  • Q&A focus broadened from last quarter’s heavier emphasis on tokenization and buybacks toward AI product launches ("Terra" and "AI Price 2.0") and specific partnership commercialization steps (Kalshi viewer/API; MAXEX). (CEO & Director Hult)

Risks and Concerns

  • "History has shown that activity can moderate as clients digest a forward outlook" after volatility spikes, and management highlighted an April pause: "clients take a bit of a breather in April." (CEO & Director Hult)
  • U.S. credit and Treasuries had channel headwinds: "revenues were down over 20% year-over-year" in the retail corporate credit channel and Treasuries faced "weaker retail trends." (CEO & Director Hult)
  • Reported credit share optics were flagged as distorted by "affiliate trades" that are "double-counted noneconomic trades," with management saying, "If you adjust for that activity, the underlying picture looks better." (CEO & Director Hult)
  • Expense variability included FX impacts: "$8.1 million of unfavorable movements in FX" and "a $5.1 million loss" in Q1 2026 vs. a prior-year gain. (Chief Financial Officer Furber)

Final Takeaway

Tradeweb management emphasized a record Q1 with $618 million in revenue, broad-based international growth, and record performance across rates, credit, money markets, and equities while pointing to continued retail weakness in U.S. credit and Treasuries. Management highlighted that April activity moderated but still tracked toward a top five revenue month, and raised its 2026 spending posture within the existing $1.1 billion to $1.16 billion adjusted expense range by saying expenses should trend toward the top half as it invests in AI (including planned Q2 launches for "Terra" and "AI Price 2.0"), credit, international expansion, ICD, and digital assets partnerships including Canton and Kalshi.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: Trump says U.S. reviewing troop reduction in Germany
data.description:
The U.S. is weighing a potential reduction of its troop presence in Germany, President Donald Trump said Thursday, signaling a possible shift in military posture amid rising tensions with Berlin over the Iran conflict.

In a post on Truth Social, Trump said Washington is “studying and reviewing” options to scale back forces, adding that a decision could "over the next short period of time."

Trump did not provide specifics on the scope or timeline of the review. However, a reduction would likely unsettle NATO allies, who rely heavily on the U.S. military presence for regional security.

As of December 2025, the U.S. currently maintained just over 36,000 active-duty personnel across roughly 20 bases in Germany, according to Defense Manpower Data Center figures, making it the largest American military footprint in Europe.

Key installations include Ramstein Air Base, a central hub for U.S. operations across Europe, Africa, and the Middle East, and Grafenwoehr Training Area, the U.S. Army’s largest training facility in the region.

The comments come amid deteriorating relations with German Chancellor Friedrich Merz. Tensions escalated after Merz criticized Washington earlier this week, saying the U.S. was being “humiliated” by Iran during ongoing, but faltering, negotiations.

Trump hit back at Merz’s remarks, posting on Truth Social that Merz appeared to think it was “OK for Iran to have a Nuclear Weapon. He doesn’t know what he’s talking about!”

“No wonder Germany is doing so poorly, both Economically, and otherwise!” Trump commented.

Diplomatic efforts, meanwhile, appear stalled. The White House has rejected an Iranian proposal tied to reopening the Strait of Hormuz in exchange for lifting U.S. blockade of Iranian ports, press secretary Karoline Leavitt confirmed, further complicating efforts to de-escalate the conflict.

data.title: Amazon, Google, and Microsoft are seeing unprecedented gains in cloud, thanks to AI
data.description:
The big three cloud service providers, Amazon (AMZN), Google (GOOG) (GOOGL), and Microsoft (MSFT), demonstrated to investors that heavy spending on artificial intelligence is paying off in spades, as evidenced by their latest quarterly results.

Azure boom

For the period ending March 31, Microsoft said it earned an adjusted $4.27 per share as revenue rose 18% year-over-year to $82.89B. Included in that was $34.7B from its Intelligent Cloud division, which consists of its Azure cloud unit. Microsoft said Azure revenue grew 40% year-over-year and 39% in constant currency.

On its earnings call, Microsoft said it expects Azure's growth rate to be 40%, above the 37% analysts were expecting and the second quarter in a row of 40% growth, as broad and growing customer demand continues to exceed supply. 

In addition, Microsoft said it is still balancing the amount of supply it can allocate internally versus other high ROI priorities. The company also said it expects roughly $190B in capex spending in 2026.

Google Cloud is skyrocketing

Google parent Alphabet saw an even stronger growth rate, as Google Cloud revenue surged 63% year-over-year to $20B, surpassing that mark for the first time.

The Sundar Pichai-led company also noted that its cloud backlog nearly doubled sequentially to $462B, amid surging enterprise AI demand. It also said that AI is now the primary growth driver for its cloud unit and that revenue from products built on its AI models skyrocketed nearly 800% year-over-year.  

The tech giant also upped its fiscal 2026 capex spending plans, as it now expects to spend between $180B and $190B, compared to the prior guidance of $175B to $185B. Additionally, Alphabet said it expects 2027 capex to “significantly increase” when compared to 2026 levels, as the AI opportunity is larger than first thought. 

The reemergence of the 800-pound gorilla

Some industry watchers have thought that AWS, often referred to as the 800-pound gorilla of the cloud computing market, lagged Azure and Google Cloud for its technical prowess when it came to AI.

Not anymore.

Revenue attributed to AWS came in at $37.6B for the first-quarter, up 28% year-over-year, which marked its best growth rate since the middle of 2022. 

Amazon CEO Andy Jassy echoed that narrative shift, as he highlighted the reasons that customers like OpenAI (OPENAI) and Anthropic (ANTHRO) are picking Amazon for their AI needs.

Jassy also said he thinks the high level of AWS spending will be monetized well and justify the short-term negative impact on free cash flow.

data.title: Biggest stock movers Thursday: GOOG, META, QCOM, and more
data.description:
Stock futures slipped on Thursday morning as geopolitical tension resurfaced, driving Brent crude to a four-year high. Investors reacted cautiously to reports that the Trump administration is weighing fresh military contingencies against Iran following a breakdown in recent diplomatic efforts.

Here are some of Thursday's biggest stock movers:

Biggest stock gainers

  • Qualcomm (QCOM) +13% - Shares surged after reporting FQ2 results that topped expectations, with adjusted EPS of $2.65 beating estimates, while revenue of $10.6B came in broadly in line despite a 3% Y/Y decline. Growth in automotive revenue (+38%) and IoT (+9%) helped offset a 13% drop in handset sales, while QTL licensing revenue rose 4.8%. Looking ahead, the company guided Q3 revenue to a midpoint of $9.6B and EPS to $2.20, both below consensus estimates, signaling near-term softness. Management noted the quarter could mark a bottom in China Android demand and highlighted growing opportunities in automotive and data center markets driven by AI-related demand, particularly in areas such as autonomous driving technologies and cloud computing services.
  • Carvana (CVNA) +9% - Shares gained after reporting a strong Q1 with record revenue, adjusted EBITDA, and net income, marking its sixth consecutive quarter of 40% Y/Y unit growth. Retail units sold climbed 40% to 187,393, while revenue jumped 52% to $6.43B and total gross profit rose 37% to $1.27B, both beating expectations. Adjusted EBITDA increased 38% to $672M, above estimates, though margins declined, with net income margin at 6.3% and EBITDA margin at 10.4% due to higher costs. The company expects sequential growth in retail units and EBITDA in Q2 and reiterated its long-term goal of reaching 3M annual car sales at a 13.5% EBITDA margin.
  • Alphabet (GOOG) (GOOGL) +7% - Shares advanced after delivering a strong Q1 beat, with revenue rising 22% Y/Y to $109.9B, ahead of estimates, marking its 11th straight quarter of double-digit growth. Net income surged 81% to $62.6B, while operating income climbed 30% with margins expanding to 36%. Cloud remained a standout, with revenue up 63% and operating income tripling, highlighting continued AI-driven momentum across the business. The company also raised its quarterly dividend by 5% to $0.22 per share, reinforcing confidence in cash flow strength.

Biggest stock losers

  • Teladoc Health (TDOC) -11% - Shares plunged after reporting mixed Q1 results and a weaker Q2 revenue outlook of $597M to $626M, below consensus. Q1 revenue beat expectations but declined 2% Y/Y, weighed by a 9% drop in the BetterHelp segment. The company reaffirmed its 2026 guidance, with revenue projected at $2.481B–$2.576B and a net loss range of ($1.05)–($0.75), broadly in line with consensus, offering little upside for investors.
  • Meta Platforms (META) -8% - Shares slid despite a strong Q1 beat, as investors focused on softer forward signals and a sharp increase in capital spending. Revenue rose 33% to $56.3B, with advertising and Family of Apps both showing strong growth, while EPS of $10.44 surged 62% YoY and beat estimates. However, sentiment was weighed down by a $4.03B loss in Reality Labs, a decline in daily active people, and higher capex expectations. The company now expects $125B–$145B in capex while guiding Q2 revenue to $58B–$61B, roughly in line with estimates, and maintaining full-year expense guidance.

data.title: Qualcomm's gains on data center entry put spotlight on ETFs with stock exposure
data.description:
Qualcomm (QCOM) on Wednesday reported Q2 results largely in line with expectations, while its Q3 guidance missed estimates, but shares surged on optimism around new data center opportunities.

Q2 non-GAAP EPS came in at $2.65, beating estimates; revenue declined 3.6% to $10.59B, roughly in line. The Q3 outlook of $9.2B to $10.0B revenue and $2.10 to $2.30 EPS trailed consensus.

It was Qualcomm's expansion into data centers that turned investor attention as it said it secured a "leading hyperscaler" as a customer, shipping its custom chips from the December quarter.

“We are excited by our entry into the data center,” said CEO Cristiano Amon, adding that multiple hyperscaler and AI-related opportunities are underway."Development of our leading data center CPU and high-performance AI inference accelerators is progressing well," CEO said in earnings call. 

Shares are up +11% in premarket trading on Thursday, putting investor focus on ETFs with significant exposure to the chipmaker, with the following 20 funds listing the stock among their top 15 holdings.

  • First Trust Nasdaq Semiconductor ETF (FTXL) 6.48% allocation
  • Direxion Daily QCOM Bull 2X ETF (QCMU) 5.99% allocation
  • SoFi Agentic AI ETF (AGIQ) 5.11% allocation
  • Invesco Next Gen Media and Gaming ETF (GGME) 4.85% allocation
  • Fidelity Metaverse ETF (FMET) 4.50% allocation
  • VanEck Fabless Semiconductor ETF (SMHX) 4.24% allocation 
  • Schwab US Dividend Equity ETF (SCHD) 4.21% allocation
  • YieldMax U.S. Stocks Target Double Distribution ETF (DDDD) 4.19% allocation 
  • VistaShares Target 15 TEPRTantrum Contrarian Distribution ETF (TPRY) 4.08% allocation 
  • First Trust NASDAQ Technology Dividend Index Fund (TDIV) 3.94% allocation
  • Vest Technology Dividend Target Income ETF (TDVI) 3.91% allocation 
  • ProShares Metaverse ETF (VERS) 3.90% allocation 
  • First Trust New Constructs Core Earnings Leaders ETF (FTCE) 3.86% allocation 
  • iShares Core Dividend ETF (DIVB) 3.83% allocation 
  • Matrix Advisors Value ETF (MAVF) 3.73% allocation
  • VictoryShares US Multi-Factor Minimum Volatility ETF (VSMV) 3.69% allocation 
  • Monarch Dividend Plus ETF (MDPL) 3.69% allocationAdditional funds that include Qualcomm are VanEck Semiconductor ETF (SMH), Madison Covered Call ETF (CVRD), Horizon Dividend Income ETF (DIVN), etc.
